title: Two audits in one day
summary: OpenAI shipped the GPT-5.6 family — Sol, Terra, Luna — and on the same day published an audit finding roughly 30% of SWE-bench Pro's tasks broken, retracting its own recommendation of the benchmark it happens to be losing on. Plus Illinois becomes the first state to require independent third-party safety audits of frontier AI systems, and a sweep from Grok 4.5 to Texas permitting data centers like dry cleaners.

topics: OpenAI GPT-5.6 family (Sol, Terra, Luna) GA July 9: all three share a 1M-token context window, 128k max output, Feb 16 2026 knowledge cutoff; pricing per 1M tokens Luna $1/$6, Terra $2.50/$15, Sol $5/$30; OpenAI reports Sol at 53.6 on Agents' Last Exam, 13.1 points above Claude Fable 5, with Terra and Luna also beating Fable 5; but Fable 5 beats Sol on SWE-bench Pro 80% vs 64.6%; Sol claimed SOTA at 80 on Artificial Analysis Coding Agent Index (+2.8 over Fable 5); Altman claims 54% more token efficiency on coding; company calls it its strongest cybersecurity model yet; adds 'max' reasoning and 'ultra' subagent modes; Simon Willison (early access) found it 'definitely very competent' but not yet better than Claude Fable on his complex coding tasks

OpenAI audit of SWE-bench Pro: automated datapoint-analysis pipeline flagged 200 broken tasks (27.4%); human annotation campaign with five experienced software engineers found 249 (34.1%); two main breakage categories are overly strict tests enforcing implementation details absent from the prompt (rejecting functionally correct patches) and underspecified prompts omitting requirements the hidden tests enforce; OpenAI retracts its previous recommendation that the community use SWE-bench Pro as a leading coding eval; published the same day GPT-5.6 posted 64.6% vs Fable 5's 80% on that benchmark; HN criticism: conflict-of-interest timing, and no cleaned subset of the working 70% was released

Illinois Artificial Intelligence Safety Measures Act: effective January 1, 2027; bipartisan passage, backed by Anthropic; requires covered large developers to identify, disclose and mitigate risks, publicly disclose safety practices, report significant safety incidents within 72 hours (matching New York; California allows 15 days), and provides confidential reporting channels and whistleblower protections; first state to mandate regular independent third-party safety audits by qualified auditors without financial conflicts of interest; 'covered' thresholds not specified in the reporting

threads.name: Frontier-model release gating
threads.status: Illinois signed the Artificial Intelligence Safety Measures Act (effective Jan 1, 2027), the first state law mandating independent third-party safety audits of covered AI systems, plus 72-hour incident reporting and whistleblower protections; Anthropic backed it. Meanwhile OpenAI shipped GPT-5.6 to general availability and published its principles for government and national-security partnerships.
threads.arc: From federal ad hoc bans and allow-lists, to the White House's classified cyber-benchmark deadline, to a state writing an independent auditor into statute — oversight moving from a government switch, to company self-commitment, to a legally required outside reader.

threads.name: Custom AI silicon vs Nvidia
threads.status: Meta's custom AI chips enter production in September on a modular chiplet design, and DeepSeek is reportedly pursuing its own chip — joining OpenAI/Broadcom and Anthropic's Samsung talks.
threads.arc: What began as two labs hedging against Nvidia is becoming the default posture for every frontier lab with the capital to try it.

dives.why_it_matters: Anyone choosing a model for agentic work now has three price tiers at one context envelope and a benchmark picture that flips depending on the ruler — Sol leads Agents' Last Exam by 13.1 points while losing SWE-bench Pro by more than 15 — so the selection decision has to be made on their own workload, not the launch post.

dives.story: OpenAI's audit and retraction of SWE-bench Pro
dives.why_it_matters: Every team that has cited a SWE-bench Pro number to pick a coding model has been reading a ruler where roughly a third of the marks are wrong in a directional way, punishing functionally correct patches that differ from the historical commit.

dives.story: Illinois Artificial Intelligence Safety Measures Act
dives.why_it_matters: Frontier labs and the compliance teams around them now face the first legally mandated independent third-party safety audit in the US, on an eighteen-month clock, for a profession of qualified conflict-free auditors that does not yet exist.

concepts_taught: how SWE-bench-style coding benchmarks are graded, and how overly strict tests plus underspecified prompts bias scoring against correct-but-unexpected patches
